sentences.text: Yes.
Mhm.
A very dull day.
We got up in the morning.
We're trying to find some--
Early, late, there was nothing to rush for.
There was nothing to do.
We had no jobs, no-- we had nothing.
But it was just trying to find to eat.
Clothes we had very little because we went to a-- we had-- we had to leave everything when we left.
And I went to work because I went-- I was picked up in buses, like, with my girlfriend who lived in same apartment.
She helped me to get that job at the airport.
And we were working.
In the evening, we were coming back.
And my parents were there with my brothers.
I think my brothers were working in a-- there was a-- they were calling that Gemeinschaft.
It was clothing-making or whatever.
I really didn't know too much about because I was busy on the other side.
Through my girlfriend I got the job.
Oh, much better.
I wasn't hungry because there were-- the Air, Air Force was feeding us.
And the job wasn't bad because we just had to clean the offices, the girls.
And that's it.
I didn't have what to trade.
That was the problem because we had to live the way we were.
Yes, yes.
I, I tell you honestly, in Poland, a, a girl like I was, I didn't know too much about the whole thing.
But I knew they were-- he had a baby.
The baby was born and midwife delivered.
And after when was the birth, eight days later, the rabbi came and he said, it has to be named Shalom for peace.
So she named it.
And the baby got typhoid.
They took him to a hospital.
And came the German woman, [SOBBING] killed it.
[CRYING] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The baby was already three, four months old.
[SOBBING] And we knew about.
I wasn't too long in the ghetto because I was at the airport.
And after that, we went to the-- Plaszow.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And in Plaszow, I was going out to work.
I was always lucky that I got a job-- I always working.
Like with Schindler, he had, at the time, not ammunition factory, but he had a pots-and-pans factory.
So I working there.
And I really wasn't working in pots and pans because they started already with ammunition.
So I was lucky enough.
I was very good-looking at the time.
So I got boyfriends.
One Juden helped me.
The other one helped me.
And we were-- I was working on the ammunition at Schindler's.
He was taking us back to Plaszow.
No, I'm sorry.
I think he had a residence for us made over there where we were working.
So I was separated with my parents, but I could see them after when I was going-- coming to the ghetto.
But it was tough in the ghetto.
My parents were still in the ghetto.
Till the--
After, from the ghetto, they were taken away.
1940-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] '41--
--end of '41, maybe?
Oh, less, less than a year.
When I went to Plaszow, no, my, my parents never went to Plaszow.
Because--
Because they were giving out, like, blue cards, green cards.
And there were a segregation of the Jewish people.
So with the blue cards, you were going to Plaszow.
With the green cards, you were going out, out of town.
So I-- my parents were hiding already in the ghetto because my sister-in-law's parents had a business there.
So they had the keys.
And they were hiding.
And I was with them.
But I said to my mother, if you all stay here, we were [INAUDIBLE].
One has to go out to look for work.
So I went out and looking-- and went-- when I went out, a German caught me and pushed me away.
I didn't know where I'm going.
But when he pushed me, I found myself in-- I ran after the people who were going-- at the airport.
So I went with them on the bus.
I was lucky.
That's all it really was.
I went back in the evening-- that they told me-- my mother heard that the German took me someplace.
And she was sure that he shot me.
So my mother and father, my sister-in-law, [SOBBING] and the two brothers went [CRYING] to the-- [SOBBING] to the group of people who were going already.
I don't know where.
I don't know where.
In buildings, just like that.
Oh, we had furniture in the ghetto.
But it was-- at the time when they started to pick up the Jews, nothing was anymore-- we didn't need nothing.
We just had to hide that they wouldn't find you.
So my parents were lucky because-- knew for-- that my sister-in-law's parents had that factory over there, the same where the ghetto was.
And they went to hide over there.
So it was sleeping on floors or whatever.
And I went out.
And there, I didn't know how I survive-- found myself completely different.
But somebody reported to my mother-- that I found out later that they caught me.
So mother my mother and father said, what are we waiting for?
Let's go out.
We will choke anyway here.
And we would have what to eat.
So they, they didn't realize what's going on.
And they reported to the transport.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I got a little card from my father, who-- he must have dropped from the train.
We're going.
We don't know where-- and it-- some other-- some other words-- a little card.
And I haven't got it because after that, I had throw away-- everything away.
No, I didn't.
No.
We knew only that whoever went with the transport never came back.
But what's going on-- nothing.
On this one?
I don't know on which one.
Or--
Oh, sure, [SIGHS] ghetto was awful.
They make it-- they made it right away smaller because there was some inspection coming on them, too.
So the ghetto was smaller when we were coming back from work.
But when I came back from work, there was nobody there anymore.
No.
We were all sure that we're going to die.
There was no future.
There was not something to look forward that, OK, tomorrow will be better.
Or everybody expect, OK, this one went today.
Till-- tomorrow, somebody else will go.
We were prepared for it.
I just survived because luck.
I wasn't smarter than somebody else.
I admit that-- just lucky.
No.
I heard in Plaszow.
And I found myself in Plaszow.
Mm-hmm.
To be scared what tonight, tomorrow, or, or this minute, maybe they're coming-- hear some noises.
That was the living.
I'll be honest with you.
I didn't been too long there in Plaszow because I was, again, lucky enough that I was chosen to go to Schindler to do work in the-- with the pots and pans over there.
So I lived over there.
And I don't know.
Oh, Plaszow was [SIGHS] just like a hell.
That was right-- a mountain-- they're up in a mountain and killing every day more people, more people.
People were going to work, but majority-- every day you could hear it.

Like my own husband, they took them, and-- for no reason, they took him from, from the barrack where he was sleeping and put him in a hall where the same day, they killed 200, 300 people and-- naked already in the hall to be shot.
And somebody said to the German, is-- there be a inspection?
Those people have to go out because we're having-- they'll find out that we killing everybody.
So-- went out.
And my husband was between them, got dressed, came back to Plaszow-- just lucky.
Did you meet your husband in Plaszow?
No, at Schindler in Brunnlitz.
But I knew him before the war.
I was working for Schindler, like I said, with the pots and pans.
How long I was in Plaszow?
Not too long, maybe six months, five months.
I really don't remember.
Yes.
No, lucky, just lucky-- just luck.
Lucky-- I remember when I went to Brunnlitz with Schindler.
It was again an Appell.
I'm sure by now you know what means 'Appell,' when they put together the Jews.
And they started to count and the-- then came out names.
So I had my girlfriend standing beside me, who lives now in, in Montreal.
And she said to me, Esther, you, you know the guy who made the list?
I knew him, a Jewish fellow who was helping make the list for Schindler.
Do you recall his name?
Goldberg.
And he was-- he wasn't too nice to the Jewish people after.
Some of them-- to me, he was nice.
And suddenly, they called my name.
And I went to Plaszow-- to Brunnlitz.
Or why and what-- people, whatever they had, you know, on their body, they were giving away to that Goldberg to be on that list because we heard that Schindler maybe will save you.
We really didn't know exactly who he is.
And I was called to go.
And I went.
My parents were not there anymore.
I didn't know where my brothers-- I was just alone.
But I had a nature, you know, I wouldn't go-- I go a straight line.
I wouldn't go-- this-- my destiny takes me here.
Maybe it's good.
And that, I believed in it.
When I was working in-- with pots and pans, he was coming every day.
He was coming.
He was checking.
And after we were starting on ammunition-- and he had inspection on him.
He wasn't-- it wasn't so easy for him, either, because the were-- they were-- the Gestapo coming and checking if he's doing his job.
But he was a nice guy.
He was very clever-- very clever.
At the beginning, yeah.
And after that, he made some living quarters for us.
And I had-- I don't know how many people could have been there, 500 maybe-- young people.
And following that did you go back to Plaszow yes because from Plaszow, I went to Brunnlitz.
I wouldn't remember that.
Not too long.
Not too long.
Again, I was very lucky.
A month or something.
I really don't remember exactly.
I don't want to put dates or what because I don't remember.
The Untersturmfuhrer of Plaszow was Amon 'Go-oth.'
Goeth.
Goeth.
I have his picture.
Oh, [SIGHS] if I would meet him, I wouldn't be talking to you.
Whoever met him, that was it.
Very-- no, I didn't.
I was a lucky one.
Oh, yeah.
If he was-- if he had to pass by us-- as a matter of fact, he hanged two of my good friends, two boys who had started to run away from Plaszow.
And they were caught.
Up And we had to pass by to see how they're hanging.
One was Oneg Fuchs.
And the other one, I don't remember.
But one I remember for sure-- beautiful two boys, just beautiful.
And they're each about 17, 18-- gorgeous boys.
No.
But that's-- I don't know we knew.
We knew about Yom Kippur.
We were fasting.
I don't know [LAUGHS] with what-- we had enough to fast.
But we-- but we knew.
I don't know how that we find out.
That, I don't recall.
I went to Brunnlitz with Schindler-- Schindler's List.
We all went-- not to Brunnlitz, to Auschwitz.
And in Auschwitz, we were about three and a half weeks.
No.
I went-- Schindler took me out.
No, I can't-- I cannot tell you exactly how long.
I was working from the Plaszow and from Emalia.
But that had nothing to do because that was a list what they made for him, who is going, who is not going.
And I was lucky enough that I went.
No, I didn't.
Like I told you before, I didn't.
My girlfriend said, do you know-- Marcel Goldberg was his name.
Ask him to put-- that's-- I heard it's very good, which she didn't know what she was talking.
And I said, you know, let me-- let me rest.
I don't know.
I don't push myself.
And when I finished-- like I said, I don't like to repeat myself.
So that-- called my name, and I went to, to Brunnlitz-- to Auschwitz.
In Auschwitz, we were hoping that the promises will be there that he would take us out.
And he did-- after three and a half weeks, he did.
And nobody from us got killed.
And there were elderly womens.
There were-- one child, as a matter of fact, we were hiding, too, a little girl, what she went with us.
And she survived.
And we went to Brunnlitz.
And he had a factory for us over there.
We were working.
We were working, but nothing was going out.
You know, he used to say to us-- I have to put a nice word for him.
Tomorrow, the inspection is coming, so you better watch it.
Because if they catch you not, not-- doing nothing, I have problems.
And he was-- if he could throw a birthday party for his wife, I wasn't at the birthday party.
But he threw a piece of bread for us, more than usually.
And sometimes, he shared three times a year the birthday party.
He was very good to us.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] If not Schindler, I wouldn't be talking to you, to Vancouver, that's for sure.
One of the--
One of the older mans, or whatever, couples, or whatever.
I was standing in a lineup.
You know, they usually put us together-- and counting.
And then my name was called to go there.
And that's it.
My friends, all the people who were there.
I don't know if we were all the prisoners, but the majority of the prisoners were kind of out.
What we-- I really didn't know-- I knew maybe I would save myself.
But when I-- we came to Auschwitz in the middle of the night and German Shepherds waiting with lights on you, you think the world is coming to end.
So no Schindler saved you.
Nobody saved you.
By train.
We're going to die.
We knew, we realized that we are going to Auschwitz.
When we see trains.
This was already 1944-- November, 1944.
Yes, but we have to pass Auschwitz.
But who believed anything?
So we-- the train was going.
We didn't know where it's going.
But when it stopped, the train-- middle of the night, darkness, and just the the lights were shining on you from every corner.
No bathroom on the train for three four days, nothing to eat.
And we landed.
German shepherds were all surrounded.
Nobody could-- there was no question of running away.
So he-- and we were designated to all kinds of barracks.
We knew already.
We knew.
We knew about concentration camps.
That's-- that you're going to die.
How did you know this?
Just it was spread.
People maybe came back with something.
Everybody knew already. 1944, there was-- whoever's away, and lots of people died already.
That was just the end of-- like of the city Cracow.
And it was a big-- I think 60,000 Jews or something that was in in Cracow.
Can you describe the events that followed after they opened the doors to the train at Auschwitz?
Like I said, the German Shepherd would greet you.
And the lights in your face and-- pitch dark at night.
And after they line us up, in the designated barracks for was, the barracks was just wood and rats from every corner.
So, like myself, I'm scared of mouse, and I'm scared of rats.
So I, I don't know from where I got the stick.
So for as long I was in Auschwitz, I was sleeping like that.
Everybody knew me.
I was doing that, so nothing would touch me.
And we were not working.
Just one day they were caught-- some people who had to give blood for the German Army.
I was lucky enough, again, I didn't.
I will give blood because they were just taking blood as long-- as soon-- she fainted or whatever, he fainted.
They stopped.
No, but woman-- but they were-- I'm sorry, it was only women.
Auschwitz was all women.
My husband went to Gross Rosen.
To, to Brunnlitz?
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] 350?
Could it be possible?
I think so.
Oh, and majority give blood-- a majority.
Very few-- I had to clean the barracks.
So I didn't go.
And I-- they didn't know where they're going, where they are taking.
So when they came back-- so then you were lucky.
You, you didn't give blood.
We all give blood.
So-- but the blood was good for them.
But the Jews were no good.
The Jewish blood was good.
They needed blood for the army.
They told us make us ready-- ready with whatever we had.
And we had line-ups.
And we were taking-- how did we go to-- we-- you were asking me about leaving Auschwitz, yes?
Again by train, I think, but we were going-- we knew where we going, that Schindler is taking us out.
I don't know.
I really do not know.
But we knew that maybe we would be safe here.
That was-- excuse me-- that was exactly November the 10th.
And we were liberated in Brunnlitz, 1945.
April.
No, no.
Oh, just happy.
We didn't know how long the happiness is going to last.
But happy that least no German Shepherd and no sticks, and nobody is beating us.
You know, I-- [INAUDIBLE].
I don't remember.
Yes, was the factory there.
We had to work in the factory.
But we knew that nothing is going-- know, looked like we are very busy.
And we were not busy.
We were doing nothing, just sitting at that chair and doing.
Like, my machine was three feet taller than I was.
And I, I couldn't reach to the whole thing.
But Schindler knew when they come to check on him.
He was very clever.
And he goes, you sit and show that you work there, do something.
Well, nothing came out from there, nothing.
Ammunition with Schindler.
He was making ammunition for them supposedly.
Hoping.
That-- for freedom, that that's-- we, we had the feeling that the end is coming.
I don't know why.
But we had the feeling that the end is coming.
But from where, from-- I really don't know.
But we knew that Cracow was taken already-- January.
I remember like now, January I knew German had to leave because Cracow was taken already.
I had-- I worked for about a week in his kitchen.
And the communication was-- he was a heavy smoker.
And I can show you he smoked like that, holding the cigarette and dropping-- one puff and dropped the cigarette.
And I picked it up.
And there, I learned how to smoke.
I never smoked in my life.
[LAUGHS]
Yeah, he was talking to us.
He-- you know, he was very friendly, very-- good guy.
I beg your pardon.
What was it?
Yes.
And he was smart enough-- 'they'-- the Gestapo.
He was smart enough, like, I know by fact that he was drinking.
They, they put some Gestapos what he didn't like it.
But he knew that they were going to report him.
So he was drinking.
And on the back of the-- they-- he report them.
How can they watch the Jews if they, they are drinking?
So they-- the inspection came, and they took them all away, the mean ones.
And so he had to free them already to be with us.
He, he really wanted to save us.
Mrs.
Kaufman, we're at the end of this tape.
And we'll continue in a moment.
Thank--
Thank you.
Thank you.
